**The following was generated by Claude AI for *planning purposes*. All ideas are my own, just put into Claude's words.**

Theme - hidden infrastructure for society
One - HackTJ 13.0 Planning Doc
The Idea
One is an omniscient panic button for emergencies when you can't speak. Hold for 3 seconds → silently streams audio, video, and location to authorities in real-time. AI triages and asks questions via text. Evidence is immediately server-side and can't be deleted.
The killer feature: When 100 people trigger it during a school shooting, AI aggregates all feeds, shows dispatchers exactly where the threat is, who's hurt, which rooms are clear. It's a dam, not a wall - controls the flood so humans can do their job.
Tagline: One second. One push. One community.

The Problem
80% of 911 calls are from mobile phones, but the system was designed for landlines
Silent emergencies (domestic violence, school shootings, home invasions) are underserved - if you can't speak, you can't get help
Dispatchers are overwhelmed and understaffed
Uvalde stats:
Two dispatchers for the entire county
400 officers on scene
70 minutes before entering the classroom
A 10-year-old called 911 and whispered "please, I don't want to die" - waited 40 minutes

Target Users
Students in school shootings
Domestic violence victims who can't speak
Hospital staff facing violence
Elderly in medical emergencies
Anyone in a situation where they can't call 911

Go-to-Market
Campus police - universities have budget, contained populations, their own dispatch
Hospitals, DV orgs, elder care - institutions who fear liability
Long-term: Native phone feature (like Fall Detection on Apple Watch)

Tech Stack
Expo - mobile app (React Native)
Cloudflare RealtimeKit - video/audio streaming, chat, transcription, recording
Next.js on Vercel - dashboard + API routes
Groq - fast AI triage (Whisper for transcription, Llama for categorization)

App Features
Mobile App (Expo)
Local storage profile (name, DOB, medical conditions, emergency contacts) - no auth
Big panic button with 3-second hold + haptic feedback
Streams video/audio/location via RealtimeKit
Receives text-based questions from AI (silent by default)
"Are you safe to speak?" prompt before going voice
Ghost Mode: Calculator disguise - fully functional calculator, type "911" + hold equals to trigger
Dispatcher Dashboard (Next.js)
Real-time alerts with video feeds, location on map
AI-generated triage summaries and priority scores
Command prompt at bottom for mass actions:
"ask all: are you safe?" → sends to all active alerts
"summarize building A" → AI aggregates status
Multiple alert handling - not one at a time

Demo Flow (60 seconds)
Hook (10 sec): "In Uvalde, a 10-year-old called 911 and whispered 'please, I don't want to die.' Two dispatchers. 40 minutes. She waited."
Transition (5 sec): "What if she had this?"
Demo (30-40 sec):
Press button on phone
Dashboard lights up - video streaming, location pinned, AI triages
Teammates press their buttons - multiple alerts appear
Type command: "ask all: are you safe?" - phones receive question
Close (5 sec): "Two dispatchers couldn't save her. One can."

Slides (5 max)
Title - "One" + tagline
Problem - Uvalde stat, quote, "911 fails when you can't speak"
Demo - switch to live demo on laptop
How it works - simple visual (AI triage, mass handling)
Close - "One. One push. That's it."

HackTJ Categories
Platform: Web (dashboard is the centerpiece)
Topic: Lifestyle (personal safety)
Opt-in: AI/ML (Groq triage)
Auto-considered: Best Overall, Social Impact, Design
Target prizes:
Best Overall (M4 MacBook Air)
Social Impact
Best AI/ML
Best Lifestyle

Team Roles
You:
Code everything
Run the live demo
Answer technical Q&A
Teammates:
Write and rehearse pitch script
Design slides
Time the presentation
Be the "other phones" in the demo (press buttons)
Handle non-technical Q&A
Keep you from feature creeping at 3am

Schedule Awareness
Saturday:
9:00 AM - Doors open
11:00 AM - Opening ceremony
11:30 AM - Hacking begins
11:30 PM - Category submission deadline (HARD)
10:00 PM - Cory Levy fireside chat (ZFellows)
Sunday:
2:00 AM → 3:00 AM - Daylight savings (lose an hour)
8:00 AM - Final submission, hacking ends
8:15 AM - Judging begins (boardroom, 3 judges, timer, slides)
11:30 AM - Results
12:00 PM - Closing ceremony

Judging Format
Boardroom with 3 judges
Timer + slideshow on HackTJ computer
Can use YOUR laptop for live demo
Phones for triggering alerts
Time includes Q&A - keep presentation to ~2 min, leave room for questions

Build Order (Suggested)
Hours 1-3: Expo app scaffold, local storage profile, panic button UI with haptics
Hours 4-6: RealtimeKit integration - get video streaming working from phone
Hours 7-9: Next.js dashboard - display incoming alerts, video feed, map
Hours 10-12: Groq integration - transcription + AI triage
Hours 13-16: Polish UI, command prompt, multi-alert handling
Hours 17-20: Ghost mode (calculator disguise), testing full demo flow
Hours 21-24: Final polish, practice demo with team, sleep buffer

What NOT to Do
Don't add features after midnight - polish only
Don't spend more than 15 min on auth (local storage, no Clerk needed)
Don't build Twilio call integration - mention it, don't build it
Don't over-explain in the pitch - let the demo speak
Don't read slides - talk over them

Objection Handling (Q&A Prep)
"So it's just a panic button app?" No - it live streams to dispatchers, AI triages, handles mass emergencies, works silently. Show me another that does all that.
"Can't you just call 911?" Not if you can't speak. DV, school shootings, home invasions. 911 asks "what's your emergency?" and you can't answer.
"AI can't replace dispatchers." It's not replacing them. It's a dam, not a wall. AI handles the flood so dispatchers can do their job with better information.
"What about privacy/legal?" Evidence is streamed server-side, encrypted, retained only for emergency response. HIPAA compliance on roadmap. Working with existing NG911 infrastructure.
"This won't actually work / you're just a high schooler." NG911 is rolling out nationwide - built for multimedia and rich data. We're building to where infrastructure is going. Start with campus police, prove it, scale.
